name: Classical Runge-Kutta 4th-order ODE integrator
principles: weighted slope sampling at intermediate points
conventions: applied to the initial-value problem y'(t) = f(t, y), y(t_0) = y_0
h is the (uniform) time step; non-uniform stepping requires adaptive variants (RK45, Dormand-Prince)
explicit method: y_{n+1} computed from y_n without solving an implicit equation
vector-valued y is supported elementwise; cost is 4 function evaluations per step
the (1, 2, 2, 1)/6 weights are the canonical 'classical RK4'; other 4th-order RK methods exist with different node patterns
no built-in error estimate; pair with a 5th-order companion (RK45) for adaptive step-size control
expectedLimits: local truncation error per step: O(h^5)
global truncation error after T/h steps: O(h^4) — fourth-order convergence
smooth f satisfying a Lipschitz condition: convergence is guaranteed for sufficiently small h
stability region: bounded subset of the complex h * lambda plane (for the linear test problem y' = lambda y); explicit methods cannot be A-stable
stiff systems (widely separated eigenvalues): RK4's stability constraint forces impractically small h — use implicit methods (BDF, RadauIIA) instead
round-off-limited regime: at very small h, accumulated round-off error overtakes truncation error; optimal h depends on machine epsilon and the problem (~ epsilon_machine^(1/5) for RK4)
references: Runge (1895) — Über die numerische Auflösung von Differentialgleichungen, Math. Ann.
Kutta (1901) — Beitrag zur näherungsweisen Integration totaler Differentialgleichungen, Z. Math. Phys.
Hairer, Norsett & Wanner — Solving Ordinary Differential Equations I: Nonstiff Problems, ch.II
Press, Teukolsky, Vetterling & Flannery — Numerical Recipes, ch.17
